title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text: Welcome to STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics!

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Quick Start

Course Schedule - Weekly schedule and course materials
Course Syllabus - Detailed course information and policies
Lectures - All course lectures organized by week
Assessments - Quizzes, exams, and projects
Resources - Textbook, R downloads, and reference materials

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Overview
STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics (4 units) introduces descriptive statistics, probability, random variables, distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, regression, and ANOVA with emphasis on applications in biological and natural resource sciences.
Lecture: Monday, Wednesday, Friday, 11:00–11:50 AM, BSS 166
Lab: Thursday (select one section: 9:00–10:50 AM, 11:00–12:50 PM, or 1:00–2:50 PM), FH 202
General Education: Lower Division GE Area 2 (Quantitative Reasoning)

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Structure
Includes collaborative daily warm-ups, weekly programming labs, weekly quizzes, two midterms, and five portfolio ready projects. Optional final exam may replace one midterm grade.

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Instructor Information
Instructor: Dr. Rosanna Overholser
Office: BSS 334
Email: rho3@humboldt.edu
Office Hours: Mondays 2-3pm, Wednesdays 12-12:50pm, Fridays 10-10:50am, or by appointment

title: Lab 4
text:
Topics: Lectures 9–11 — permutations and combinations; coin tosses and the binomial random process; dbinom, pbinom, and rbinom; simulation; Bayes’ rule (PPV).






NoteOpen in Google Colab (R)




Open demo in Colab
Open student exercise in Colab

After a notebook opens, use Runtime → Change runtime type and select R if needed. Save a copy to your Drive (File → Save a copy in Drive) before editing the student notebook.






Demo notebook (lab-4-demo.ipynb) — walk through in Thursday lab with your instructor. Two main examples:

Bayes’ rule via simulation (screening test / PPV)
Coin tosses and binomial functions — dbinom, pbinom, rbinom, simulation, and simple ggplot bar plots

Student notebook (lab-4-student.ipynb) — complete the prompts and turn in your reflection on Canvas by Tuesday (see Assessments).





By the end of this lab, you should be able to:

Compute factorials, permutations, and combinations in R (factorial(), prod(), choose()).
Identify \(n\), \(p\), and \(x\) for a binomial count and compute \(P(X = x)\) with dbinom().
Use pbinom() for cumulative and tail probabilities (watch lower.tail and the cutoff).
Simulate binomial outcomes with rbinom() and with a for loop + sample().
Compare simulated proportions to exact binomial probabilities on a bar plot (geom_bar() with after_stat(prop) vs geom_col() with dbinom).
Mark observed data on a binomial plot with geom_vline() and interpret a tail-area p-value.
Estimate \(P(D \mid T^+)\) by simulation and check with Bayes’ rule.








factorial(n) — compute \(n!\)
prod(1:n) — another way to compute \(n!\)
choose(n, k) — combinations \({}_nC_k\)





dbinom(x, size = n, prob = p) — \(P(X = x)\)
pbinom(q, size = n, prob = p) — \(P(X \leq q)\) (adjust lower.tail for upper tails)
rbinom(n_sims, size = n, prob = p) — simulate counts of successes in \(n\) trials





ggplot(), aes() — map variables to axes
geom_bar() with after_stat(count) or after_stat(prop) — bar charts from raw data
geom_col() — bars from precomputed heights (e.g. dbinom probabilities)
geom_vline(xintercept = ...) — vertical reference line at observed \(x\)






The student exercise has 11 parts plus a short reflection:



Part
Topic




1
Factorials (factorial, prod)


2
Permutations \({}_{10}P_5\)


3
Combinations \({}_{10}C_5\)


4
Binomial model — free throws; dbinom


5
Cumulative probabilities — pbinom


6
Simulation with rbinom


7
Coin toss simulation with a for loop


8
Plot simulated proportions vs dbinom bars


9
Vertical line + tail area (simulation vs pbinom)


10
Bayes’ rule (PPV) via simulation


11
Sampling distribution picture (pre–Project 1 intuition)








What parts of this lab were the hardest for you?
Do you still have questions on any of the problems? If so, what are they?





Part 11 builds the binomial distribution plot and tail probability you will use in Project 1. Lab 5 is a precheck for your own research question and hypotheses.

title: Lab 6
text:
Topics: Lectures 15–16 — mean and SD of a binomial count (\(\mu = np\), \(\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)}\)); normal approximation to the binomial; two-sided tests; when \(np \geq 10\) and \(n(1-p) \geq 10\) matter.






NoteOpen in Google Colab (R)




Open demo in Colab — worked solutions for instructor walk-through
Open student exercise in Colab

After a notebook opens, use Runtime → Change runtime type and select R if needed. Save a copy to your Drive (File → Save a copy in Drive) before editing the student notebook.






Demo notebook (lab-6-demo.ipynb) — completed examples to follow in Thursday lab (coin and die with poor normal approximation; large-\(n\) coin with good approximation).
Student notebook (lab-6-student.ipynb) — fill in the prompts for the same three problems and turn in on Canvas by Tuesday (see Assessments).





By the end of this lab, you should be able to:

Test \(H_0: p = p_0\) vs \(H_a: p \neq p_0\) (two-sided) for a binomial random process.
Plot the null distribution with dbinom() and geom_col(); add a vertical line at the data and shade the two-sided p-value region.
Compute a two-sided p-value with pbinom().
Compute center \(\mu = np\) and spread \(\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)}\); add labeled vertical lines at \(\mu\), \(\mu \pm 1\sigma\), and \(\mu \pm 2\sigma\) on the binomial plot.
Plot the normal approximation on the same \(x\)-scale with dnorm(); mark the observed data.
Decide whether the data fall in a rejection region (e.g. beyond \(2\sigma\) from \(\mu\)) and state a conclusion in context.
Recognize when the normal approximation is poor vs good (\(np \geq 10\) and \(n(1-p) \geq 10\)).








dbinom(x, size = n, prob = p) — \(P(X = x)\) under \(H_0\)
pbinom(q, size = n, prob = p) — cumulative probabilities for p-values (watch lower.tail for two-sided tails)





dnorm(x, mean = mu, sd = sigma) — normal curve heights for overlay plots
Conditions: \(np \geq 10\) and \(n(1-p) \geq 10\) (check before trusting the approximation)





geom_col() — binomial bar chart from a data frame of \(x\) and dbinom probabilities
geom_vline(xintercept = ...) — mark observed \(x\) or reference lines at \(\mu \pm k\sigma\)
geom_line() — normal approximation curve
aes(fill = ...) — shade bars in the two-sided tail regions














Problem
Setting
Normal approx.




1
Fair coin, \(n = 10\), observe 1 head
Poor (\(np\) and \(n(1-p)\) both \(< 10\))


2
Fair die (six sides), \(n = 12\), observe 0 sixes
Poor


3
Fair coin, large \(n\), observe data near center
Good (conditions met)



Each problem walks through parts (a)–(f): parameters and \(\mu\), \(\sigma\) → binomial plot with shading → pbinom p-value → center/spread lines on binomial plot → normal overlay → rejection region and conclusion.




This lab bridges Project 1 (exact binomial test) and later one-proportion inference. It prepares you for Midterm 1 (Week 7) material on binomial counts, normal approximation, and hypothesis testing language.

title: Lab 8
text:
Topics: Week 9 — describing variables with the tidyverse; numerical vs categorical summaries; filter() and group_by() + summarise(). Builds skills used in Lab 9 and Project 3.






NoteOpen in Google Colab (R)




Open demo in Colab — walk-through with the mpg dataset
Open student exercise in Colab — prompts with the diamonds dataset

After a notebook opens, use Runtime → Change runtime type and select R if needed. Save a copy to your Drive (File → Save a copy in Drive) before editing the student notebook.






Demo notebook (lab-8-demo.ipynb) — follow along in Thursday lab using mpg (highway MPG, drive type, vehicle class, cylinders).
Student notebook (lab-8-student.ipynb) — complete five questions on diamonds and turn in on Canvas by Tuesday (see Assessments).





By the end of this lab, you should be able to:

Load a tidyverse dataset and inspect variables with glimpse().
Identify whether a variable is numerical or categorical (class(), levels()).
Recode a variable when numeric values are really category codes (factor(), mutate()).
For one numerical variable: compute mean and SD and make a histogram.
For one categorical variable: compute count and proportion by level.
Use filter() to subset rows with conditions.
Use group_by() with summarise() to compare groups (mean, SD, n()).








Function
What it does




library(tidyverse)
Loads core tidyverse packages


glimpse(df)
Quick view of names and types


class(x), levels(x)
Type and category levels


mutate(), factor()
Create or recode variables


mean(), sd()
Center and spread for numerical data


count(), summarise(), n()
Counts and grouped summaries


filter()
Keep rows matching conditions


group_by()
Define groups before summarizing


ggplot() + geom_histogram()
Histogram for numerical data


as.numeric(), as.factor()
Convert between types














Part
Topic




1
Numerical variable — hwy: type, mean, SD, histogram


2
Categorical variable — drv: levels, counts, proportions


3
Type change — treat cyl as categories


4
filter() — subset by MPG and drive type


5
group_by() + summarise() — mean highway MPG by drv and by class










Question
Topic




1
Numerical — carat: type, mean, SD, histogram


2
Categorical — cut: levels, counts, proportions


3
Type change — color as codes → factor


4
filter() — large carat and Premium/Ideal cut


5
group_by() — price summaries by cut, sorted by mean







Project 2 is due Sunday, March 29. Before or during this lab, review the sample write-up and notebooks:

Sample PDF · HTML example
Mean + FPC notebook · Colab
Proportion + FPC notebook · Colab

Use Lab 7 for the five confidence-interval situations (Wald, Plus Four, FPC for mean and proportion).




Lab 9 extends these tidyverse workflows on diamonds with more practice before Project 3 (descriptive analysis of a TidyTuesday-style dataset).

title: Project 1
text:
Total points: 35
Due: Sunday, Week 5 (see Assessments and Canvas)
In this project, you will write a one-page summary of how you used the binomial distribution to draw a conclusion about an infinite process from data you collected. For the inference to be valid, your data must satisfy the four conditions of a binomial random process (BRP). The steps below guide you through planning, collecting data, analyzing in R, and writing the report.
Sample write-up: Download sample one-page write-up — ESP binomial test. This is my completed report for an ESP study. Do not copy my process (ESP is a class example you may not use for your own project). Write up your own study following the grading rubric below.



Please follow these steps before Thursday, Feb. 19:

THINK of a binomial random process you could observe by Thursday, Feb. 19. Carefully consider each of the four BRP conditions until you are sure each holds for your chosen process. Do not use a process from a class lecture example, such as coin tosses or ESP. The four conditions are: (a) binary outcome in each trial (success or failure), (b) independent outcomes between trials, (c) fixed probability of success in a single trial (\(p\)), and (d) fixed number of trials (\(n\)).
BEFORE you observe the process, make your best guess about whether \(p\), the probability of one success in a single trial, is larger than, smaller than, or not equal to some number. Write your guess as null and alternative hypotheses:
\(H_0\): \(p\) = some number
\(H_a\): \(p\) (> or < or \(\neq\)) some number
The value for \(p\) must be a number between 0 and 1.
Be ready to share your two hypotheses in class Thursday, Feb. 19 so you can make a plot of what to expect (step 4) in lab.
OBSERVE \(n = 25\) trials from your chosen process. Record how many successes occurred (\(x\)). Compute the observed proportion \(\hat{p} = x/n\).
In R, use dbinom() and geom_col() to create a bar plot of \(P(X = x)\) for \(x = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n\) using your \(n\) and the value of \(p\) from \(H_0\). Use geom_col() with exact binomial probabilities from dbinom(), not geom_bar() on simulated data.
Add a vertical line at your observed \(x\) with geom_vline(xintercept = ...). Compute the p-value: the probability of observing a new value of \(X\) as extreme as your \(x\), assuming \(H_0\) is true. Use pbinom() (or equivalent) and match the direction of your alternative hypothesis.
Based on the size of your p-value, decide whether \(H_0\) is consistent with your data. A small p-value means your observed \(x\) is far from where new data would most likely fall if your \(H_0\) value of \(p\) were correct.
You will have time in class on Thursday, Feb. 19 to create your plot and p-value. See also Lab 5 (precheck) and the Lecture 12 binomial test Colab notebook.
Write up your study in a one-page summary using the section headings in the rubric below. Include your R figures (descriptive bar/pie chart and binomial distribution plot with your data marked).





Total points: 35


Your summary should be organized into 4 sections (1 section with 2 subsections) with the following headings:

Introduction

Data Collection

Data Analysis

3a. Descriptive Statistics

3b. Inferential Statistics


Conclusion





Your summary should fit within one side of an 8.5 by 11 inch page. (1 point)
Your summary should be concise and written in complete sentences with proper grammar. (2 points)




In this section, you should:

Briefly describe what question you are trying to answer. (1 point)

Briefly describe the motivation you have for trying to answer the question. (1 point)

Describe the parameter of interest \(p\) in one sentence. (1 point)

State the null and alternative hypotheses for your research question using appropriate notation and a value for \(p\). (2 points)





Describe how, when, and where you collected data in enough detail that someone else could repeat your study. (3 points)
Explicitly explain why you believe each of the four BRP conditions was satisfied by your method. (4 points) Explain in the context of your study, not as a generic list of the four conditions.





Include the total number of trials \(n\), the number of successes \(x\), and the percentage of successes you observed. (3 points)
Include a well-labelled pie chart or bar chart of your observed data, made in R (e.g. with ggplot2). (3 points)




In this subsection, please include:

A graph of the binomial distribution for all possible values of \(X\) using the \(p\) from \(H_0\). Create this as a column chart in R using dbinom() for the bar heights and geom_col(). (4 points)
The p-value: the probability of seeing a new \(X\) as extreme as your \(x\) if \(H_0\) is true. Compute it in R with pbinom() (or an equivalent correct method). (2 points)
A brief explanation of how you calculated the p-value. (1 point)
A clear indication of where your data \(x\) appears on the binomial graph (e.g. a vertical line or label). (1 point)

See the ESP sample PDF for an example of wording and figures.




Write a one-sentence summary of what your p-value means in the context of your research question and data. (2 points) (See the sample project for an example.)
Based on the size of this p-value, state what you conclude about your research question. (2 points)

title: Project 2 — Example: Mean age of California high schools
text:
This page is a worked example for the structure of Project 2. Your submission must use your own population, sampling plan, and variable — not this one.
Download: Sample one-page write-up with histogram (PDF)
Sample notebooks (same CA high schools context):

Mean with FPC: project-2-estimation-mean-fpc.ipynb — Open in Colab
Proportion with FPC: project-2-proportion-fpc.ipynb — Open in Colab




What is the mean age of high schools in California? As someone who teaches college-level classes, I was interested in learning more about the population of schools my students might come from. One question is how old those schools tend to be: I think older schools might tend to have more resources and stronger connections with a community, whereas newer schools might have less resources.
Parameter: mean age (in years, in 2024) of all California high schools.
Population: high schools listed in the California School Directory.






On April 3, 2024, I visited www.cde.ca.gov/SchoolDirectory and searched for any type of high school. I found \(N = 1617\) schools.
I used the random number generator on RossmanChance.com to choose \(n = 10\) numbers from 1 to 1617. I located each corresponding school by setting the number of schools shown on a page to 100, going to the page with the right hundreds value, and counting up or down to find the school matching each random number’s hundreds and ones digits.
For each of the 10 schools, I looked up the Open Date and calculated age in 2024 by subtracting the open year from 2024. My variable is numerical (age in years).



I used the survey package in R to compute a 95% confidence \(t\)-interval with finite population correction for the mean age of all CA high schools, using the ages from my sample of 10 schools. Step-by-step code is in the mean notebook.
Conditions for this interval:

a simple random sample from a numbered list of the population, and

belief that the population distribution of ages is roughly symmetric (checked with a histogram).








The mean age of the ten schools was 35 years and the standard deviation was 25 years. See Figure 1 in the sample PDF (histogram of ages).



Based on my dataset, I am 95% confident that the mean age of all CA high schools is between 20 and 51 years old in 2024.





The mean age of high schools in CA was younger than I expected. Because my sample of 10 schools was chosen with a simple random sampling scheme, I do not expect my estimate to be biased. However, \(n = 10\) is small, so the estimate is not very precise: there is about a 31-year spread between the lower and upper bounds of the interval. I could improve precision by increasing the sample size.




The proportion notebook uses the same school ages but estimates the proportion of CA high schools that are more than 50 years old in 2024 — a second Project 2-style analysis with a binary outcome and FPC.

title: Project 2 — Example: Mean age of California high schools
text:
What is the mean age of high schools in California? As someone who teaches college-level classes, I was interested in learning more about the population of schools my students might come from. One question is how old those schools tend to be: I think older schools might tend to have more resources and stronger connections with a community, whereas newer schools might have less resources.
Parameter: mean age (in years, in 2024) of all California high schools.
Population: high schools listed in the California School Directory.

For each of the 10 schools, I looked up the Open Date and calculated age in 2024 by subtracting the open year from 2024. My variable is numerical (age in years).



I used the survey package in R to compute a 95% confidence \(t\)-interval with finite population correction for the mean age of all CA high schools, using the ages from my sample of 10 schools. Step-by-step code is in the mean notebook.
Conditions for this interval:

belief that the population distribution of ages is roughly symmetric (checked with a histogram).

title: Project 2
text:
Total points: 35
Due: Sunday, March 29 (Week 9; see Assessments and Canvas)
In this project, you will use a 95% confidence interval to estimate a population proportion or population mean using data collected from a simple random sample. Complete the steps below, then submit a one-page summary and an R notebook that runs top to bottom.
Sample write-up: Download sample one-page write-up — CA high schools (PDF). HTML version: Project 2 — Example: mean age of California high schools. Do not copy my population or variable — write up your own study following the grading rubric below.
Sample R notebooks (adapt to your data; choose mean or proportion):



Goal
Notebook
Open in Colab




Estimate a population mean with FPC
project-2-estimation-mean-fpc.ipynb
Open in Colab


Estimate a population proportion with FPC
project-2-proportion-fpc.ipynb
Open in Colab



After opening in Colab, use Runtime → Change runtime type and select R if needed.






Date
Milestone




Thursday, March 12
R code and simple random sampling introduced in Lab 7


Thursday, March 26
Peer review of sampling method and interval choice in lab


Sunday, March 29
One-page summary and R notebook due in Canvas








One-page summary (PDF or equivalent) — organized using the section headings in the rubric below.
R notebook (.ipynb, .qmd, or .Rmd) — all code to load your data, make figures, and compute your 95% confidence interval. The notebook must run from top to bottom.







Think of a population you could list, randomly sample from the list, and visit (or inspect) each sampled item to collect one variable — either a number or a binary outcome.
It is hard to randomly sample people without university research approval and without a complete contact list for the population. For these reasons, I recommend a population of non-human objects. For example:

Number all parking spaces in a lot (e.g. from a Google Maps image), randomly select 10 spaces, and record one variable at each space (occupied or not? truck or not? white or not? model year?).
Sample from a numbered list of items on a website, as in the sample project.

Your turn.
My population is ________________________________, which has \(N =\) ______ objects.



Once you have a population, decide what mean or proportion you want to estimate. Identify the variable you will collect for each sampled object.
Your turn.
My variable is ________________________, which is ☐ binary categorical ☐ numerical (circle one),
so I’ll be estimating a ☐ proportion ☐ mean (circle one).



Larger samples give more precision, but collecting data takes time. In professional work, a sample size calculation balances time and precision. For this project, collect data from 10 objects or the largest number you can feasibly visit in 1–2 hours — whichever you choose as your sample size.
Your turn.
I think the largest number of objects I could feasibly visit and collect information for is \(n =\) ______.



Use a random number generator to select \(n\) distinct items from \(1\) to \(N\). We have used this one in class:
Rossman Chance random number generator
Your turn.
The \(n\) items I need to visit are: ________________________________



Visit each sampled item and record your variable. Keep your dataset in R (a vector or a small data frame).
Your turn.
The dataset from my sample is: ________________________________



Compute the sample mean (numerical variable) or sample proportion (binary variable). This is your single best estimate of the population parameter.
Your turn.
My single best estimate of the population is __________________



Decide which of the five situations from Lecture 19 applies to your data:

Finite \(N\)? Multiply the standard error by the finite population correction (FPC) factor \(\sqrt{(N-n)/(N-1)}\).
Mean? Use a \(t\)-interval (or t.test, or survey with an SRS design).
Proportion? Use a one-proportion \(z\)-interval (Wald) if the sample size is large enough, or a binomial method (binom.test) if the sample size is small.

Record and check the conditions for your chosen interval, then compute it using R. See Lab 7 or start from the mean notebook or proportion notebook above.
Your turn.
My 95% confidence interval estimate is \(L =\) ______ and \(U =\) ______.
The conditions for this interval are:





Write one sentence using this recipe:

I am 95% confident that the _______________ (parameter: mean or proportion) of _______________ (variable) for _______________ (population) is between ______ (\(L\)) and ______ (\(U\)).




Follow the grading rubric below. Include your R figures (e.g. histogram or bar chart) and your interval interpretation.





Total points: 35



Briefly describe in a single sentence the parameter (mean or proportion) you are estimating.
Clearly state the population associated with this parameter.
Briefly describe your motivation for choosing to estimate this quantity.







Include a concise description of your population, including its size \(N\).
Describe how you obtained a simple random sample from a list of the population (e.g. random number generator and population size).
Describe exactly how you located an item corresponding to each random number (e.g. “I set the number of schools shown on a page to 100…”).
Describe the variable you collected from each sampled item. State clearly whether you collected a number or a binary outcome. If numerical, include units.





Describe how you computed the 95% confidence interval in R (e.g. survey, t.test, prop.test, or binom.test).
State whether you used the FPC.
Describe the conditions for your selected interval and whether you believe they are met.








Include a well-labelled graph of your dataset.
Include the mean and SD if the variable is numerical, or the count and proportion if the variable is binary categorical.





Include the values of \(L\) and \(U\) from your 95% confidence interval.
Include a one-sentence interpretation of the interval in the context of your parameter and population.






Briefly describe your reaction to the final estimate — is it about what you expected?
Briefly state whether you believe your estimate is biased.
Briefly state how you could make your estimate more precise.
